name: pipeline-ordering
description: Tests whether the model places sampling first in the pipeline (before formatting)
prompt: Set up a slog pipeline in Go using samber/slog libraries with PII scrubbing (mask email fields), error routing to Sentry, and log sampling at 10%. Show the complete handler composition using slog-multi, slog-sampling, and slog-formatter.
trap: Without the skill, the model places sampling last (after formatting) or in the middle, wasting CPU on records that get dropped

assertions.text: Sampling is the outermost/first handler in the pipeline (applied before formatting)
Uses slog-sampling library (slogsampling) for sampling, not custom code
Uses slog-formatter for PII scrubbing (PIIFormatter or FormatByKey)
Uses slogmulti.Router or Fanout for routing to Sentry
Explains or demonstrates why sampling should be first (avoid wasted CPU on dropped records)

name: fanout-vs-router
description: Tests whether the model picks Router over Fanout for level-based routing
prompt: I want errors to go to Sentry, all logs to go to stdout as JSON, and warnings to go to Slack. Set this up using samber/slog-multi in Go. Which handler composition pattern should I use?
trap: Without the skill, the model uses Fanout for everything — Slack receives all records including debug/info, not just warnings

assertions.text: Uses slogmulti.Router() for level-based routing (not Fanout for all three)
stdout/JSON handler receives all log levels
Sentry handler receives only errors (uses LevelIs(slog.LevelError) or equivalent predicate)
Slack handler receives only warnings (uses LevelIs(slog.LevelWarn) or equivalent predicate)
Explains the distinction between Router (predicate-based) and Fanout (broadcast all)

name: missing-close-batch
description: Tests whether the model remembers to call Close() on batch handlers
prompt: Set up slog to send logs to Datadog using samber/slog-datadog in a Go HTTP server with graceful shutdown. Show the complete main() function.
trap: Without the skill, the model forgets to close the handler on shutdown, causing buffered logs to be lost

assertions.text: Calls handler.Stop(ctx) or defer handler.Stop(ctx) for the Datadog handler (not Close)
Uses Option{}.NewDatadogHandler() constructor pattern
Close happens during graceful shutdown (signal handling or defer in main)
Mentions or demonstrates that Datadog handler batches logs (data loss risk without Close)
Import path is github.com/samber/slog-datadog/v2 or later

name: sampling-strategy-selection
description: Tests whether the model picks the right sampling strategy and exempts errors
prompt: My Go service processes 50k requests/sec. Logging costs are $2k/month. I need to reduce log volume by 90% but keep all error and warning logs intact. Which samber/slog-sampling strategy should I use? Show the setup.
trap: Without the skill, the model uses UniformSamplingOption which drops errors too, or implements custom sampling logic instead of using the library

assertions.text: Uses ThresholdSamplingOption or AbsoluteSamplingOption (not UniformSamplingOption)
Configures sampling to only affect Debug/Info levels, not Warn/Error
Warn and Error records pass through unsampled (via Router or Custom sampler)
Uses samber/slog-sampling library, not custom sampling code
Explains why Uniform sampling is wrong for this use case (it drops errors too)

name: router-missing-default
description: Tests whether the model identifies missing catch-all handler in Router
prompt:
I have a slog-multi Router that routes ERROR to Sentry and INFO to Loki. But my DEBUG logs are disappearing entirely — they don't appear anywhere. Here's my setup:

```go
logger := slog.New(
    slogmulti.Router().
        Add(sentryHandler, slogmulti.LevelIs(slog.LevelError)).
        Add(lokiHandler, slogmulti.LevelIs(slog.LevelInfo)).
        Handler(),
)
```

What's wrong?
trap: Without the skill, the model suggests checking the log level threshold or Loki configuration, not the missing default handler in the Router

assertions.text: Identifies the missing catch-all/default handler in the Router as the root cause
Explains that Router silently drops records matching no predicate
Suggests adding a handler without a predicate (catch-all) or adding LevelIs(slog.LevelDebug)
Uses slogmulti.Router() API correctly in the fix
Does NOT suggest changing the global log level as the primary fix

name: http-middleware-filters
description: Tests whether the model uses slog-gin's Config with filters instead of custom middleware
prompt: Add request logging to my Gin server using samber/slog-gin. I want to skip logging for /health and /metrics endpoints, and include request bodies for debugging. Show the setup.
trap: Without the skill, the model uses a generic custom Gin middleware or manually filters paths instead of using slog-gin's built-in Config and Filters

assertions.text: Uses sloggin.NewWithConfig() (not just sloggin.New()) to pass configuration
Configures Filters with IgnorePath for /health and /metrics
Sets WithRequestBody: true in the Config
Config includes DefaultLevel, ClientErrorLevel, and/or ServerErrorLevel fields
Uses sloggin.IgnorePath or sloggin.IgnorePathPrefix (not a custom filter function)

name: pipe-middleware-chain
description: Tests whether the model uses Pipe middleware instead of custom Handler wrapper
prompt: I need to inject a trace_id from OpenTelemetry context and scrub email addresses from all log records before they reach any handler. I'm using samber/slog-multi. Show me how to set this up as reusable middleware.
trap: Without the skill, the model creates a custom slog.Handler wrapper struct instead of using slogmulti.Pipe with inline middleware

assertions.text: Uses slogmulti.Pipe() for middleware chaining
Creates middleware for trace_id injection (inline middleware or AttrFromContext)
Creates middleware or formatter for email/PII scrubbing
Pipe wraps the final sink handler(s)
Does NOT implement a custom slog.Handler struct with Enabled/Handle/WithAttrs/WithGroup methods

name: failover-handler
description: Tests whether the model uses Failover instead of custom retry logic
prompt: My slog pipeline sends logs to Loki over the network, but Loki has occasional outages lasting 1-5 minutes. I want to automatically fall back to local file logging when Loki is unavailable, then resume Loki when it's back. Show the setup using samber/slog-multi.
trap: Without the skill, the model builds custom retry/fallback logic with goroutines and channels instead of using slog-multi's Failover handler

assertions.text: Uses slogmulti.Failover() handler
Loki handler is the primary (first) handler in the Failover chain
File/local handler is the fallback (second) handler
Does NOT implement custom retry/fallback logic with goroutines or channels
Uses slog-loki library for the Loki handler (not a custom HTTP client)

name: pool-vs-fanout-latency
description: Tests whether the model recommends Pool over Fanout for latency-sensitive pipelines
prompt: My slog Fanout to 5 remote handlers is slowing down my HTTP responses. Each handler takes 5-50ms to write. The total latency added per request is 25-250ms because Fanout processes them sequentially. How do I fix this latency problem while keeping all 5 destinations? Show the code change.
trap: Without the skill, the model suggests spawning goroutines per log record or using buffered channels instead of slog-multi's Pool handler

assertions.text: Identifies sequential processing in Fanout as the root cause
Recommends replacing Fanout with slogmulti.Pool() for concurrent broadcast
Explains that Pool dispatches to all handlers concurrently, reducing latency from sum-of-all to max-of-all handler duration
Does NOT suggest spawning raw goroutines per log record as the primary solution
Shows the code change from slogmulti.Fanout(handlers...) to slogmulti.Pool()(handlers...)

name: formatter-pii-scrubbing
description: Tests whether the model uses slog-formatter as Pipe middleware for cross-cutting PII scrubbing
prompt: I need to ensure no email addresses or IP addresses appear in any log output from my Go service. We use slog with slog-multi routing to 3 different handlers (stdout, Loki, Sentry). Show me how to add PII protection that applies to ALL handlers in one place.
trap: Without the skill, the model adds per-handler filtering or regex on output instead of using slog-formatter as a Pipe middleware wrapping all downstream handlers

assertions.text: Uses slog-formatter library (slogformatter package)
Uses PIIFormatter and/or IPAddressFormatter from slog-formatter
Applies formatter as a Pipe middleware wrapping all downstream handlers (not per-handler)
Formatter is applied once in the pipeline, before the Router/Fanout
Does NOT implement custom regex-based filtering or per-handler PII logic

name: backend-option-pattern
description: Tests whether the model uses correct Option{} constructor pattern for backend handlers
prompt: Set up slog to send error-level logs to Sentry and all logs to Grafana Loki using samber/slog-sentry and samber/slog-loki. Show the complete setup with correct imports and handler creation.
trap: Without the skill, the model uses incorrect constructor patterns (e.g., New() function, slogsentry.New(), or positional args) instead of the Option{}.NewXxxHandler() pattern

assertions.text: Uses slogsentry.Option{}.NewSentryHandler() constructor pattern
Uses slogloki.Option{}.NewLokiHandler() constructor pattern
Uses slogmulti.Router() for level-based routing (errors to Sentry, all to Loki)
Import paths use versioned modules (github.com/samber/slog-sentry/v2, github.com/samber/slog-loki/v3)
Calls lokiClient.Stop() or defer lokiClient.Stop() for graceful shutdown (flush buffered logs)

name: attrfromcontext-without-middleware
description: Tests whether the model identifies that AttrFromContext needs HTTP middleware to populate context
trap: Without the skill, the model debugs context.Value key types or suggests adding context.WithValue manually in every handler, instead of identifying the missing HTTP middleware that injects attributes

assertions.text: Identifies that no HTTP middleware is populating the request_id into context
Recommends adding slog-gin/echo/fiber/chi middleware (or manual context injection middleware)
Explains that the HTTP middleware is what injects request attributes into context
Does NOT suggest only changing the context key type as the primary fix
Shows the connection between HTTP middleware and AttrFromContext
Mentions WithRequestID config option or equivalent for the middleware
Does NOT blame slog-multi or slog-sentry configuration as the root cause
